---
format:
  revealjs:
    css: custom.css
    transition: none
    aspect-ratio: "16:9"
---

## ECON 0150 | Economic Data Analysis {.center}
<p class="subheader">The economist's data analysis workflow.</p>

<br>

### *Part 4.2 | Categorical Predictors*

---

## GLM: The story so far
<p class="subheader">We've built two models. Now we need a third.</p>

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from scipy import stats
import pandas as pd

# Update Matplotlib parameters
plt.rcParams.update({
    'font.family': 'serif',
    'font.serif': ['Times New Roman'],
    'font.style': 'italic',
})

# Create a figure with three subplots
fig, (ax1, ax2, ax3) = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(12, 4))

# --- Panel 1: Paired differences (3.4) ---
wait_difference = [1.3, 2.4, 2.2, 1.3, 3.0, 2.3, 0.8, 2.7, 2.0, 2.9, 1.7, 1.9, 2.2,
                   2.1, 2.4, 1.7, 1.7, 1.9, 1.7, 2.5, 2.4, 2.2, 1.9, 1.9, 2.7, 1.8,
                   1.8, 2.0, 1.9, 2.1]
n_diff = len(wait_difference)
mean_diff = np.mean(wait_difference)

np.random.seed(42)
jitter_x = np.random.uniform(-0.3, 0.3, n_diff)
ax1.scatter(jitter_x, wait_difference, alpha=0.6, color='darkblue', s=30)
ax1.axhline(mean_diff, color='r', linestyle='-', linewidth=2)
ax1.set_xlim(-1, 1)
ax1.set_xticks([])
ax1.set_ylabel('Wait Difference (min)', fontsize=12)
ax1.set_title('Part 3.4: Paired\n$d = \\beta_0 + \\varepsilon$', fontsize=13)

# --- Panel 2: Continuous predictor (4.1) ---
np.random.seed(42)
n = 60
minutes_after_open = np.linspace(0, 600, n)
wait_times = 5 + 0.01 * minutes_after_open + np.random.normal(0, 2, n)
wait_times = np.maximum(0, wait_times)
slope, intercept, r_value, p_value, std_err = stats.linregress(minutes_after_open, wait_times)

ax2.scatter(minutes_after_open, wait_times, alpha=0.6, color='darkblue', s=30)
x_line = np.array([min(minutes_after_open), max(minutes_after_open)])
y_line = intercept + slope * x_line
ax2.plot(x_line, y_line, 'r-', linewidth=2)
ax2.set_xlabel('Minutes After Opening', fontsize=12)
ax2.set_ylabel('Wait Time (min)', fontsize=12)
ax2.set_title('Part 4.1: Numerical\n$y = \\beta_0 + \\beta_1 x + \\varepsilon$', fontsize=13)

# --- Panel 3: Unpaired groups (4.2 preview) ---
np.random.seed(42)
n_g = 50
low_green = 22 + np.random.normal(0, 1.5, n_g)
high_green = 19 + np.random.normal(0, 1.5, n_g)
jitter_g = np.random.uniform(-0.15, 0.15, 2*n_g)
x_groups = np.concatenate([np.zeros(n_g), np.ones(n_g)])

ax3.scatter(x_groups + jitter_g, np.concatenate([low_green, high_green]),
            alpha=0.4, color='darkblue', s=30)
ax3.set_xticks([0, 1])
ax3.set_xticklabels(['0\n(No)', '1\n(Yes)'])
ax3.set_xlabel('High Greenspace', fontsize=12)
ax3.set_ylabel('Temperature (°C)', fontsize=12)
ax3.set_xlim(-0.5, 1.5)
ax3.set_title('Part 4.2: Categorical\n$y = \\beta_0 + \\beta_1 x + \\varepsilon$ ?', fontsize=13)

sns.despine(ax=ax1, left=False, bottom=True, right=True, top=True, trim=True)
sns.despine(ax=ax2, left=False, bottom=False, right=True, top=True, trim=True)
sns.despine(ax=ax3, left=False, bottom=False, right=True, top=True, trim=True)
plt.tight_layout()
```

***Q.** Do neighborhoods with greenspace have lower temperatures?*

. . .

<br><br>

*We need a new approach, but we already have the tools.*

---

## GLM: City Greenspace and Temperature
<p class="subheader">Q. Is temperature lower in neighborhoods with more green space?</p>

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd

# Update Matplotlib parameters
plt.rcParams.update({
    'font.family': 'serif',
    'font.serif': ['Times New Roman'],
    'font.style': 'italic',
})

# Set seed for reproducibility
np.random.seed(42)

# Create 50 data points for each group
n = 50
low_green = 22 + np.random.normal(0, 1.5, n)  # Higher temperatures
high_green = 19 + np.random.normal(0, 1.5, n)  # Lower temperatures
jitter = np.random.uniform(-0.1,0.1,2*n)

# Combine into a dataframe
data = pd.DataFrame({
    'temperature': np.concatenate([low_green, high_green]),
    'high_green': np.concatenate([np.zeros(n), np.ones(n)])
})

# Plot the data
plt.figure(figsize=(11, 4))
plt.scatter(data['high_green']+jitter, data['temperature'], alpha=0.4, color='darkblue')

plt.xticks([0, 1], ['0\n(No)', '1\n(Yes)'])
plt.ylabel('Temperature (°C)', fontsize=14)
plt.xlabel('High Greenspace', fontsize=14)

plt.xlim(-0.5,1.5)
sns.despine(trim=True)
plt.tight_layout()
```

*What is the variable type of **High Greenspace**?*

. . .

***Q.** Does temperature change as we increase by one on the horizontal axis?*

. . .

$$Temperature = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot HighGreen + \varepsilon$$

---

## GLM: City Greenspace and Temperature
<p class="subheader">Q. Is temperature lower in neighborhoods with more green space?</p>

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd

# Update Matplotlib parameters
plt.rcParams.update({
    'font.family': 'serif',
    'font.serif': ['Times New Roman'],
    'font.style': 'italic',
})

# Set seed for reproducibility
np.random.seed(42)

# Create 50 data points for each group
n = 50
low_green = 22 + np.random.normal(0, 1.5, n)  # Higher temperatures
high_green = 19 + np.random.normal(0, 1.5, n)  # Lower temperatures
jitter = np.random.uniform(-0.1,0.1,2*n)

# Combine into a dataframe
data = pd.DataFrame({
    'temperature': np.concatenate([low_green, high_green]),
    'high_green': np.concatenate([np.zeros(n), np.ones(n)])
})

# Plot the data
plt.figure(figsize=(11, 4))
plt.scatter(data['high_green']+jitter, data['temperature'], alpha=0.4, color='darkblue')

plt.xticks([0, 1], ['0\n(No)', '1\n(Yes)'])
plt.ylabel('Temperature (°C)', fontsize=14)
plt.xlabel('High Greenspace', fontsize=14)

plt.xlim(-0.5,1.5)
sns.despine(trim=True)
plt.tight_layout()
```

1. The GLM framework doesn't care whether $x$ is continuous or binary. We code the group as 0 and 1, and fit the same model.

. . .

2. Like with numerical predictors, the GLM tests whether $\beta_1$ is different from zero.

---

## GLM: City Greenspace and Temperature
<p class="subheader">Q. Does temperature change as we move out on the horizontal axis?</p>

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd

# Update Matplotlib parameters
plt.rcParams.update({
    'font.family': 'serif',
    'font.serif': ['Times New Roman'],
    'font.style': 'italic',
})

# Set seed for reproducibility
np.random.seed(42)

# Create 50 data points for each group
n = 50
low_green = 22 + np.random.normal(0, 1.5, n)  # Higher temperatures
high_green = 19 + np.random.normal(0, 1.5, n)  # Lower temperatures
jitter = np.random.uniform(-0.1,0.1,2*n)

# Combine into a dataframe
data = pd.DataFrame({
    'temperature': np.concatenate([low_green, high_green]),
    'high_green': np.concatenate([np.zeros(n), np.ones(n)])
})

# Plot the data
plt.figure(figsize=(11, 4))
plt.scatter(data['high_green']+jitter, data['temperature'], alpha=0.4, color='darkblue')

# Calculate and display the means
mean_low = np.mean(low_green)
mean_high = np.mean(high_green)
plt.plot([0, 1], [mean_low, mean_high], 'r-', linewidth=2)

plt.xticks([0, 1], ['0\n(No)', '1\n(Yes)'])
plt.ylabel('Temperature (°C)', fontsize=14)
plt.xlabel('High Greenspace', fontsize=14)

plt.text(-0.35, mean_low, f'$\\beta_0$ = {mean_low:.2f}°C', fontsize=12, va='center')
plt.text(1.15, mean_high, f'$\\beta_0$ + $\\beta_1$ = {mean_high:.2f}°C', fontsize=12, va='center')
plt.text(0.5, (mean_low+mean_high)/2+0.5, f'$\\beta_1$ = {mean_high-mean_low:.2f}°C', fontsize=12, va='center', ha='center', rotation=-8)

plt.xlim(-0.5,1.5)
sns.despine(trim=True)
plt.tight_layout()
```

$$Temperature = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot HighGreen + \varepsilon$$

. . .

How would we interpret $\beta_0$ here?

. . .

*> $\beta_0$ is the mean temperature in ($x=0$) low green space cities (22.03°C)*

---

## GLM: City Greenspace and Temperature
<p class="subheader">Q. Does temperature change as we move out on the horizontal axis?</p>

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd

# Update Matplotlib parameters
plt.rcParams.update({
    'font.family': 'serif',
    'font.serif': ['Times New Roman'],
    'font.style': 'italic',
})

# Set seed for reproducibility
np.random.seed(42)

# Create 50 data points for each group
n = 50
low_green = 22 + np.random.normal(0, 1.5, n)  # Higher temperatures
high_green = 19 + np.random.normal(0, 1.5, n)  # Lower temperatures
jitter = np.random.uniform(-0.1,0.1,2*n)

# Combine into a dataframe
data = pd.DataFrame({
    'temperature': np.concatenate([low_green, high_green]),
    'high_green': np.concatenate([np.zeros(n), np.ones(n)])
})

# Plot the data
plt.figure(figsize=(11, 4))
plt.scatter(data['high_green']+jitter, data['temperature'], alpha=0.4, color='darkblue')

# Calculate and display the means
mean_low = np.mean(low_green)
mean_high = np.mean(high_green)
plt.plot([0, 1], [mean_low, mean_high], 'r-', linewidth=2)

plt.xticks([0, 1], ['0\n(No)', '1\n(Yes)'])
plt.ylabel('Temperature (°C)', fontsize=14)
plt.xlabel('High Greenspace', fontsize=14)

plt.text(-0.35, mean_low, f'$\\beta_0$ = {mean_low:.2f}°C', fontsize=12, va='center')
plt.text(1.15, mean_high, f'$\\beta_0$ + $\\beta_1$ = {mean_high:.2f}°C', fontsize=12, va='center')
plt.text(0.5, (mean_low+mean_high)/2+0.5, f'$\\beta_1$ = {mean_high-mean_low:.2f}°C', fontsize=12, va='center', ha='center', rotation=-8)

plt.xlim(-0.5,1.5)
sns.despine(trim=True)
plt.tight_layout()
```

$$Temperature = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot HighGreen + \varepsilon$$

How would we interpret $\beta_1$ here?

. . .

*> Cities with Green Space (x=1) have a temperature that is lower by $\beta_1$*

. . .

*> ie. a one unit increase in $x$ changes temperature by $\beta_1$*


---

## Categorical GLM: sampling error
<p class="subheader">Like before, if we take many samples, we get slightly different slopes and slightly different fits.</p>

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from scipy import stats

# Update Matplotlib parameters
plt.rcParams.update({
    'font.family': 'serif',
    'font.serif': ['Times New Roman'],
    'font.style': 'italic',
})

# Set seed for reproducibility
np.random.seed(42)

# Define true parameters
true_mean_low = 22
true_mean_high = 19
noise_std = 1.5
n_per_group = 50

# Function to generate a single sample dataset
def generate_sample(n=50):
    low = true_mean_low + np.random.normal(0, noise_std, n)
    high = true_mean_high + np.random.normal(0, noise_std, n)
    x = np.concatenate([np.zeros(n), np.ones(n)])
    y = np.concatenate([low, high])
    return x, y

# Generate 8 different samples and fit models
n_examples = 8
samples = []
slopes = []
intercepts = []

for i in range(n_examples):
    x, y = generate_sample()
    slope, intercept, r_value, p_value, std_err = stats.linregress(x, y)
    samples.append((x, y))
    slopes.append(slope)
    intercepts.append(intercept)

# Create subplot grid for the 8 examples
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 4, figsize=(11, 6))
axes = axes.flatten()

for i, ax in enumerate(axes):
    x, y = samples[i]
    slope = slopes[i]
    intercept = intercepts[i]

    # Jitter x for display
    jitter = np.random.uniform(-0.1, 0.1, len(x))

    # Plot data points
    ax.scatter(x + jitter, y, alpha=0.4, s=20, color='darkblue')

    # Plot regression line
    x_line = np.array([0, 1])
    y_line = intercept + slope * x_line
    ax.plot(x_line, y_line, 'r-', linewidth=2)

    # Clean up axis labels
    if i % 4 == 0:
        ax.set_ylabel('Temperature (°C)', fontsize=10)
    if i >= 4:
        ax.set_xlabel('High Greenspace', fontsize=10)
    else:
        ax.set_xlabel('')

    ax.set_xticks([0, 1])
    ax.set_xticklabels(['0', '1'])
    ax.set_xlim(-0.4, 1.4)
    ax.set_ylim(14, 27)

    # Set title
    ax.set_title(f'Sample {i+1}: $\\beta_1$ = {slope:.2f}', fontsize=11)

    sns.despine(ax=ax, left=False, bottom=False, right=True, top=True, trim=True)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

---

## Categorical GLM: sampling distribution of slopes
<p class="subheader">The slope coefficient follows a normal distribution centered on the population difference.</p>

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from scipy import stats

# Update Matplotlib parameters
plt.rcParams.update({
    'font.family': 'serif',
    'font.serif': ['Times New Roman'],
    'font.style': 'italic',
})

# Set random seed for reproducibility
np.random.seed(42)

# True parameters
true_mean_low = 22
true_mean_high = 19
true_diff = true_mean_high - true_mean_low  # -3
noise_std = 1.5
n_per_group = 50
n_simulations = 1000

# Run many simulations to get slope coefficients
slope_coefficients = []
for i in range(n_simulations):
    low = true_mean_low + np.random.normal(0, noise_std, n_per_group)
    high = true_mean_high + np.random.normal(0, noise_std, n_per_group)
    x = np.concatenate([np.zeros(n_per_group), np.ones(n_per_group)])
    y = np.concatenate([low, high])
    slope, intercept, r_value, p_value, std_err = stats.linregress(x, y)
    slope_coefficients.append(slope)

# Create the plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 4))

# Plot histogram of slope coefficients
plt.hist(slope_coefficients, bins=30, alpha=0.2, density=True,
         color='red', edgecolor='white', label='Sample Slopes')

# Overlay theoretical t-distribution
se_slope = np.std(slope_coefficients)
x_pdf = np.linspace(min(slope_coefficients), max(slope_coefficients), 1000)
y_pdf = stats.t.pdf(x_pdf, df=2*n_per_group-2, loc=true_diff, scale=se_slope)
plt.plot(x_pdf, y_pdf, color='green', linestyle='--', linewidth=2, label='Sampling Dist.')

# Add vertical line for true difference
plt.axvline(true_diff, color='black', linestyle='--', linewidth=2,
           label=f'True Difference')

plt.xlabel('Slope Coefficient: $\\beta_1$', fontsize=16)
plt.legend()
plt.yticks([])
sns.despine(left=True, bottom=False, right=True, top=True, trim=True)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

. . .

*> the slopes follow a normal distribution around the population difference!*

. . .

*> this lets us perform a t-test on the slope!*

---

## Categorical GLM: sampling distribution of slopes
<p class="subheader">The slope coefficient follows a normal distribution centered on the population difference.</p>

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from scipy import stats

# Update Matplotlib parameters
plt.rcParams.update({
    'font.family': 'serif',
    'font.serif': ['Times New Roman'],
    'font.style': 'italic',
})

# Set random seed for reproducibility
np.random.seed(42)

# True parameters
true_mean_low = 22
true_mean_high = 19
true_diff = true_mean_high - true_mean_low  # -3
noise_std = 1.5
n_per_group = 50
n_simulations = 1000

sample_slope = -2.97

# Run many simulations to get slope coefficients
slope_coefficients = []
for i in range(n_simulations):
    low = true_mean_low + np.random.normal(0, noise_std, n_per_group)
    high = true_mean_high + np.random.normal(0, noise_std, n_per_group)
    x = np.concatenate([np.zeros(n_per_group), np.ones(n_per_group)])
    y = np.concatenate([low, high])
    slope, intercept, r_value, p_value, std_err = stats.linregress(x, y)
    slope_coefficients.append(slope)

# Create the plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 4))

# Plot histogram of slope coefficients (faded)
plt.hist(slope_coefficients, bins=30, alpha=0.05, density=True,
         color='red', edgecolor='white', label='Sample Slopes')

# Overlay theoretical t-distribution
se_slope = np.std(slope_coefficients)
x_pdf = np.linspace(min(slope_coefficients), max(slope_coefficients), 1000)
y_pdf = stats.t.pdf(x_pdf, df=2*n_per_group-2, loc=true_diff, scale=se_slope)
plt.plot(x_pdf, y_pdf, color='green', linestyle='--', linewidth=2, label='Sampling Dist.')

# Add vertical line for sample slope
plt.axvline(sample_slope, color='red', linestyle='--', linewidth=2,
           label=f'Sample Slope')

plt.xlabel('Slope Coefficient: $\\beta_1$', fontsize=16)
plt.legend()
plt.yticks([])
sns.despine(left=True, bottom=False, right=True, top=True, trim=True)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

*> we don't know the entire distribution, just our sample slope*

---

## Categorical GLM: sampling distribution of slopes
<p class="subheader">The slope coefficient follows a normal distribution centered on the population difference.</p>

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from scipy import stats

# Update Matplotlib parameters
plt.rcParams.update({
    'font.family': 'serif',
    'font.serif': ['Times New Roman'],
    'font.style': 'italic',
})

# Set random seed for reproducibility
np.random.seed(42)

# True parameters
true_mean_low = 22
true_mean_high = 19
true_diff = true_mean_high - true_mean_low  # -3
noise_std = 1.5
n_per_group = 50
n_simulations = 1000

sample_slope = -2.97
null = 0

# Run many simulations to get slope coefficients
slope_coefficients = []
for i in range(n_simulations):
    low = true_mean_low + np.random.normal(0, noise_std, n_per_group)
    high = true_mean_high + np.random.normal(0, noise_std, n_per_group)
    x = np.concatenate([np.zeros(n_per_group), np.ones(n_per_group)])
    y = np.concatenate([low, high])
    slope, intercept, r_value, p_value, std_err = stats.linregress(x, y)
    slope_coefficients.append(slope)

# Create the plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 4))

# Plot histogram of slope coefficients (very faded)
plt.hist(slope_coefficients, bins=30, alpha=0.02, density=True,
         color='red', edgecolor='white')

# Overlay theoretical t-distribution centered on null
se_slope = np.std(slope_coefficients)
x_pdf = np.linspace(-6, 6, 1000)
y_pdf = stats.t.pdf(x_pdf, df=2*n_per_group-2, loc=null, scale=se_slope)
plt.plot(x_pdf, y_pdf, color='green', linestyle='--', linewidth=2, label='Sampling Dist.')

# Add vertical line for sample slope
plt.axvline(sample_slope, color='red', linestyle='--', linewidth=2,
           label=f'Sample Slope')
plt.axvline(null, color='black', linestyle='-', linewidth=2,
           label=f'Default Null')

plt.xlabel('Slope Coefficient: $\\beta_1$', fontsize=16)
plt.legend()
plt.yticks([])
sns.despine(left=True, bottom=False, right=True, top=True, trim=True)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

*> center the distribution on our null*

*> check the distance from the sample*

---

## Categorical GLM: sampling distribution of slopes
<p class="subheader">The slope coefficient follows a normal distribution centered on the population difference.</p>

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from scipy import stats

# Update Matplotlib parameters
plt.rcParams.update({
    'font.family': 'serif',
    'font.serif': ['Times New Roman'],
    'font.style': 'italic',
})

# Set random seed for reproducibility
np.random.seed(42)

# True parameters
true_mean_low = 22
true_mean_high = 19
true_diff = true_mean_high - true_mean_low  # -3
noise_std = 1.5
n_per_group = 50
n_simulations = 1000

sample_slope = -2.97
null = 0

# Run many simulations to get slope coefficients
slope_coefficients = []
for i in range(n_simulations):
    low = true_mean_low + np.random.normal(0, noise_std, n_per_group)
    high = true_mean_high + np.random.normal(0, noise_std, n_per_group)
    x = np.concatenate([np.zeros(n_per_group), np.ones(n_per_group)])
    y = np.concatenate([low, high])
    slope, intercept, r_value, p_value, std_err = stats.linregress(x, y)
    slope_coefficients.append(slope)

# Create the plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 4))

# Plot histogram of slope coefficients (very faded)
plt.hist(slope_coefficients, bins=30, alpha=0.01, density=True,
         color='red', edgecolor='white')

# Overlay theoretical t-distribution centered on null
se_slope = np.std(slope_coefficients)
x_pdf = np.linspace(-6, 6, 1000)
y_pdf = stats.t.pdf(x_pdf, df=2*n_per_group-2, loc=null, scale=se_slope)
plt.plot(x_pdf, y_pdf, color='green', linestyle='--', linewidth=2, label='Sampling Dist.')

# Add vertical line for sample slope
plt.axvline(sample_slope, color='red', linestyle='--', linewidth=2,
           label=f'Sample Slope')
# Add vertical line for null hypothesis
plt.axvline(null, color='black', linestyle='-', linewidth=2,
           label=f'Default Null')
plt.axvline(-sample_slope, color='red', linestyle='--', linewidth=2, alpha=0.3,
           label=f'Equally Extreme')

# SHADE P-VALUE REGIONS
# Left tail (beyond sample slope, which is negative)
x_left = x_pdf[x_pdf <= sample_slope]
y_left = stats.t.pdf(x_left, df=2*n_per_group-2, loc=null, scale=se_slope)
plt.fill_between(x_left, 0, y_left, alpha=0.3, color='blue', label='p-Value')

# Right tail (beyond mirror of sample slope)
x_right = x_pdf[x_pdf >= -sample_slope]
y_right = stats.t.pdf(x_right, df=2*n_per_group-2, loc=null, scale=se_slope)
plt.fill_between(x_right, 0, y_right, alpha=0.3, color='blue')

plt.xlabel('Slope Coefficient: $\\beta_1$', fontsize=16)
plt.legend()
plt.yticks([])
sns.despine(left=True, bottom=False, right=True, top=True, trim=True)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

. . .

*> p-value: the 'surprisingness' of our sample if $\beta_1 = 0$*

. . .

*> the probability of seeing our sample by chance if there is no difference*

. . .

*> a small p-value is evidence against the null hypothesis ($\beta_1 = 0$)*

---

## Categorical GLM: null slopes
<p class="subheader">Many possible slopes we might observe by chance if the null ($\beta_1 = 0$) were true.</p>

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd

# Update Matplotlib parameters
plt.rcParams.update({
    'font.family': 'serif',
    'font.serif': ['Times New Roman'],
    'font.style': 'italic',
})

# Set seed for reproducibility
np.random.seed(42)

# Create 50 data points for each group
n = 50
low_green = 22 + np.random.normal(0, 1.5, n)  # Higher temperatures
high_green = 19 + np.random.normal(0, 1.5, n)  # Lower temperatures
jitter = np.random.uniform(-0.1,0.1,2*n)

# Combine into a dataframe
data = pd.DataFrame({
    'temperature': np.concatenate([low_green, high_green]),
    'high_green': np.concatenate([np.zeros(n), np.ones(n)])
})

# Plot the data
plt.figure(figsize=(11, 4))

sns.boxplot(x='high_green', y='temperature', data=data,
            color='white',  # Use white for the boxes
            width=0.2,
            zorder=-1)

plt.scatter(data['high_green']+jitter, data['temperature'], alpha=0.4, color='darkblue')

# Calculate and display the means
mean_low = np.mean(low_green)
mean_high = np.mean(high_green)

# Calculate standard error for the difference in means
pooled_std = np.sqrt(((n-1)*np.var(low_green, ddof=1) + (n-1)*np.var(high_green, ddof=1)) / (2*n - 2))
se_diff = pooled_std * np.sqrt(1/n + 1/n)

# Draw random samples under null hypothesis (no difference)
n_samples = 100
np.random.seed(42)
for i in range(n_samples):
    # Random slope centered on 0 with appropriate standard error
    random_slope = np.random.normal(0, se_diff*1.5)
    random_high = mean_low + random_slope
    plt.plot([0, 1], [mean_low, random_high], 'grey', alpha=0.1, linewidth=0.8)

# Plot the observed difference (on top)
plt.plot([0, 1], [mean_low, mean_high], 'r-', linewidth=2, label='Observed difference')

plt.xticks([0, 1], ['0\n(No)', '1\n(Yes)'])
plt.ylabel('Temperature (°C)', fontsize=14)
plt.xlabel('High Greenspace', fontsize=14)

plt.text(-0.35, mean_low, f'$\\beta_0$ = {mean_low:.2f}°C', fontsize=12, va='center')
plt.text(1.15, mean_high, f'$\\beta_0$ + $\\beta_1$ = {mean_high:.2f}°C', fontsize=12, va='center')
plt.text(0.5, (mean_low+mean_high)/2+0.5, f'$\\beta_1$ = {mean_high-mean_low:.2f}°C', fontsize=12, va='center', ha='center', rotation=-8)

# Add text explaining the null distribution
plt.text(0.5, 23.5, 'Grey lines: possible samples under the null (no difference)',
         fontsize=10, ha='center', style='italic', color='gray')

plt.xlim(-0.5,1.5)
sns.despine(trim=True)
plt.tight_layout()
```

. . .

*> how likely does it look like this slope was drawn from the null slopes?*

. . .

*> p-value: the probability of a slope as extreme as ours under the null ($\beta_1=0$)*

---

## This is a two-sample t-test
<p class="subheader">This is a simpler view of a t-test.</p>

<br>

1. Estimating differences between group means is exactly a **two-sample t-test**.

. . .

2. GLM is a general framework that can handle in one modeling approach:

- Numerical predictors
- Categorical predictors
- Control variables (later)

. . .

3. The two-sample t-test is a special case of the GLM.

---

## Exercise: Income and Mental Health
<p class="subheader">Do individuals earning under $35,000 report more poor mental health days?</p>

**Step 1: Summarize the data**

. . .

<br>

```python
# Load BRFSS data
data = pd.read_csv('data/BRFSS_cleaned.csv')
data['low_income'] = (data['INCOME2'] <= 5).astype(int)

# Visualize Binary Predictor
sns.boxplot(data=data, x='low_income', y='MENTHLTH', color='white', width=0.2)
plt.xticks([0,1], labels=['Above $35k', 'Below $35k']  # INCOME2 <= 5)
```

. . .

```{python}
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd

# Update Matplotlib parameters
plt.rcParams.update({
    'font.family': 'serif',
    'font.serif': ['Times New Roman'],
    'font.style': 'italic',
})

# Load BRFSS data
data = pd.read_csv('data/BRFSS_cleaned.csv')
data['low_income'] = (data['INCOME2'] <= 5).astype(int)
data = data[['low_income', 'MENTHLTH']].dropna()

# Take a random subsample for cleaner visualization
np.random.seed(42)
sample = data.sample(n=200, random_state=42)
jitter = np.random.uniform(-0.15, 0.15, len(sample))

# Plot the data
plt.figure(figsize=(11, 4))
sns.boxplot(x='low_income', y='MENTHLTH', data=sample,
            color='white', width=0.2, zorder=-1)
plt.scatter(sample['low_income'] + jitter, sample['MENTHLTH'], alpha=0.4, color='darkblue')

plt.xticks([0, 1], ['Above $35k\n(0)', 'Below $35k\n(1)'])
plt.ylabel('Days of Poor Mental Health', fontsize=14)
plt.xlabel('Income Below $35,000', fontsize=14)

plt.xlim(-0.5, 1.5)
sns.despine(trim=True)
plt.tight_layout()
```


---

## Exercise: Income and Mental Health
<p class="subheader">Do individuals earning under $35,000 report more poor mental health days?</p>

**Step 2: Build a model**

. . .

$$MentalHealthDays = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot LowIncome + \varepsilon$$

---

## Exercise: Income and Mental Health
<p class="subheader">Do individuals earning under $35,000 report more poor mental health days?</p>

**Step 3: Estimate the model**

. . .

```{python}
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd

# Update Matplotlib parameters
plt.rcParams.update({
    'font.family': 'serif',
    'font.serif': ['Times New Roman'],
    'font.style': 'italic',
})

# Load BRFSS data
data = pd.read_csv('data/BRFSS_cleaned.csv')
data['low_income'] = (data['INCOME2'] <= 5).astype(int)
data = data[['low_income', 'MENTHLTH']].dropna()

# Compute group means from full data
mean_above = data[data['low_income'] == 0]['MENTHLTH'].mean()
mean_below = data[data['low_income'] == 1]['MENTHLTH'].mean()
beta_1 = mean_below - mean_above

# Take a random subsample for visualization
np.random.seed(42)
sample = data.sample(n=200, random_state=42)
jitter = np.random.uniform(-0.15, 0.15, len(sample))

# Plot the data
plt.figure(figsize=(11, 4))
sns.boxplot(x='low_income', y='MENTHLTH', data=sample,
            color='white', width=0.2, zorder=-1)
plt.scatter(sample['low_income'] + jitter, sample['MENTHLTH'], alpha=0.4, color='darkblue')

# Regression line connecting group means
plt.plot([0, 1], [mean_above, mean_below], 'r-', linewidth=2)

plt.xticks([0, 1], ['Above $35k\n(0)', 'Below $35k\n(1)'])
plt.ylabel('Days of Poor Mental Health', fontsize=14)
plt.xlabel('Income Below $35,000', fontsize=14)
plt.text(-0.4, mean_above, f'$\\beta_0$ = {mean_above:.1f}', fontsize=12, va='center')
plt.text(1.15, mean_below, f'$\\beta_0$ + $\\beta_1$ = {mean_below:.1f}', fontsize=12, va='center')
plt.text(0.5, (mean_above + mean_below)/2 + 2, f'$\\beta_1$ = {beta_1:.1f}', fontsize=12, va='center', ha='center')

plt.xlim(-0.5, 1.5)
sns.despine(trim=True)
plt.tight_layout()
```

. . .

```python
# Model: y = b + mx
model = smf.ols('MENTHLTH ~ low_income', data).fit() # Intercept is included by default
print(model.summary().tables[1])
```

. . .

- $\beta_0$ = Mean poor mental health days for those earning above $35k

- $\beta_1$ = Additional poor mental health days for those earning below $35k

---

## Exercise: Income and Mental Health
<p class="subheader">Do individuals earning under $35,000 report more poor mental health days?</p>

**Step 4: Check the residuals (next time)**

```python
sns.scatterplot(x=model.predict(), y=model.resid, alpha=0.5)
plt.axhline(y=0, color='red', linestyle='-')
plt.xlabel('Fitted Values')
plt.ylabel('Residuals')
```

. . .

*> what do you notice about this residual plot?*

---

## Exercise: Income and Mental Health
<p class="subheader">Do individuals earning under $35,000 report more poor mental health days?</p>

**Step 5: Interpret and communicate the findings**

```{python}
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd

# Update Matplotlib parameters
plt.rcParams.update({
    'font.family': 'serif',
    'font.serif': ['Times New Roman'],
    'font.style': 'italic',
})

# Load BRFSS data
data = pd.read_csv('data/BRFSS_cleaned.csv')
data['low_income'] = (data['INCOME2'] <= 5).astype(int)
data = data[['low_income', 'MENTHLTH']].dropna()

# Compute group means from full data
mean_above = data[data['low_income'] == 0]['MENTHLTH'].mean()
mean_below = data[data['low_income'] == 1]['MENTHLTH'].mean()
beta_1 = mean_below - mean_above

# Take a random subsample for visualization
np.random.seed(42)
sample = data.sample(n=200, random_state=42)
jitter = np.random.uniform(-0.15, 0.15, len(sample))

# Plot the data
plt.figure(figsize=(11, 4))
sns.boxplot(x='low_income', y='MENTHLTH', data=sample,
            color='white', width=0.2, zorder=-1)
plt.scatter(sample['low_income'] + jitter, sample['MENTHLTH'], alpha=0.4, color='darkblue')

# Regression line connecting group means
plt.plot([0, 1], [mean_above, mean_below], 'r-', linewidth=2)

plt.xticks([0, 1], ['Above $35k\n(0)', 'Below $35k\n(1)'])
plt.ylabel('Days of Poor Mental Health', fontsize=14)
plt.xlabel('Income Below $35,000', fontsize=14)
plt.text(-0.4, mean_above, f'$\\beta_0$ = {mean_above:.1f}', fontsize=12, va='center')
plt.text(1.15, mean_below, f'$\\beta_0$ + $\\beta_1$ = {mean_below:.1f}', fontsize=12, va='center')
plt.text(0.5, (mean_above + mean_below)/2 + 2, f'$\\beta_1$ = {beta_1:.1f}', fontsize=12, va='center', ha='center')

plt.xlim(-0.5, 1.5)
sns.despine(trim=True)
plt.tight_layout()
```

. . .

*> A significant positive $\beta_1$ suggests those earning under $35k report more poor mental health days per month*

. . .

*> but does low income cause poor mental health, or are other factors at play?*

---

## The General Linear Model *(so far)*
<p class="subheader">Extending the GLM framework</p>

- Part 3 | Intercept-Only Model | $y = \beta_0 + \epsilon$

- Part 4.1 | Numerical Predictor | $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \epsilon$
- Part 4.2 | Categorical Predictor | $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \epsilon$
- Part 4.3 | Model Diagnostics
- Part 4.4 | Causality

---

## Looking Forward
<p class="subheader">Extending the GLM framework</p>

Part 5 | Control Variables

$$y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + ... + \varepsilon$$

- Part 5.1 | Numerical Controls
- Part 5.2 | Categorical Controls
- Part 5.3 | Interactions
- Part 5.4 | Model Selection

*> all built on the same statistical foundation*
